annotations.label: Compact group

anchor.snippet: a compact ( topological ) group is a topological group whose topology realizes it as a compact topological space

annotations.note: Mathlib expresses a compact (topological) group idiomatically as the conjunction of typeclasses `[IsTopologicalGroup G]` and `[CompactSpace G]` (e.g. in `Mathlib.Topology.Algebra.Category.ProfiniteGrp.Basic`).

annotations.label: Basic examples of compact Lie groups

anchor.snippet: Basic examples of compact Lie groups include

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `unitaryGroup`/`specialUnitaryGroup` but does not assemble the Wikipedia list (U(1), SU(n), SO(n), Sp(n), Spin(n)) as compact Lie group examples — e.g. SO(n) and Spin(n) have no dedicated definitions.

annotations.label: Classification theorem of compact Lie groups

anchor.snippet: The classification theorem of compact Lie groups states that up to finite extensions and finite covers this exhausts the list of examples

annotations.note: No classification theorem for compact Lie groups is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Identity component of a compact Lie group

anchor.snippet: Any compact Lie group G has an identity component

annotations.note: `Subgroup.connectedComponentOfOne` defines the identity component as a subgroup of any topological group, subsuming the compact Lie case.

annotations.label: Structure of connected compact Lie groups

anchor.snippet: Every connected compact Lie group is the quotient by a finite central subgroup of a product of a simply connected compact Lie group and a torus

annotations.note: No structure theorem for connected compact Lie groups is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Decomposition of simply connected compact Lie groups

anchor.snippet: every compact, connected, simply-connected Lie group K is a product of finitely many compact, connected, simply-connected simple Lie groups

annotations.note: Mathlib has no decomposition theorem for simply-connected compact Lie groups into simple factors.

annotations.label: Correspondence with complex semisimple Lie algebras

anchor.snippet: The classification of compact, simply connected Lie groups is the same as the classification of complex semisimple Lie algebras

annotations.note: No correspondence between compact simply-connected Lie groups and complex semisimple Lie algebras is established in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maximal torus

anchor.snippet: a maximal torus , that is a subgroup T of K that is isomorphic to a product of several copies of

annotations.note: Loogle reports no `maximalTorus` declaration in Mathlib; the notion of a maximal torus of a compact Lie group is not formalized.

annotations.label: Torus theorem

anchor.snippet: A basic result is the torus theorem which states that every element of

annotations.note: The torus theorem (every element of a compact connected Lie group lies in some maximal torus) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: p-adic integers and profinite groups

anchor.snippet: any profinite group is a compact group

annotations.note: `ProfiniteGrp` is defined as compact + totally disconnected topological groups, and `PadicInt.compactSpace` (Mathlib.NumberTheory.Padics.ProperSpace) gives the p-adic integers as a compact group.

annotations.label: Pontryagin duality examples

anchor.snippet: Pontryagin duality provides a large supply of examples of compact commutative groups

annotations.note: `PontryaginDual` is defined in `Mathlib.Topology.Algebra.PontryaginDual` as continuous homomorphisms into the circle.

annotations.label: Existence of Haar measure on compact groups

anchor.snippet: Compact groups all carry a Haar measure

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.Measure.haarMeasure` constructs the Haar measure on any locally compact Hausdorff group, of which compact groups are a special case.

annotations.label: Peter–Weyl decomposition of L^2(G)

anchor.snippet: the Peter–Weyl theorem provides a decomposition of

annotations.note: Grep finds no `PeterWeyl` declaration in Mathlib; the Peter–Weyl theorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Cartan's theorem (closed subgroup)

anchor.snippet: Cartan's theorem states that Im(ρ) must itself be a Lie subgroup in the unitary group

annotations.note: Cartan's closed-subgroup theorem (a closed subgroup of a Lie group is a Lie subgroup) is not formalized in Mathlib's manifold/Lie group infrastructure.

annotations.label: Irreducible representations of a torus are one-dimensional

anchor.snippet: Schur's lemma tells us that each irreducible representation

annotations.note: Mathlib does not have a result identifying irreducible continuous representations of a torus with one-dimensional characters.

annotations.label: Analytically integral element

anchor.snippet: is called an analytically integral element

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition of an analytically integral element of the Lie algebra of a torus.

annotations.label: Irreducible representations of U(1)

anchor.snippet: Suppose, for example, T is just the group

annotations.note: The classification of irreducible representations of U(1) as z ↦ zⁿ is not stated in Mathlib (no dedicated `Circle` representation theory).

annotations.label: Weight of a representation

anchor.snippet: occurs at least once in the decomposition of the restriction of

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Mathlib/Algebra/Lie/Weights/` develops weight theory for Lie modules, but not weights of a representation of a compact Lie group obtained by restricting to a maximal torus.

annotations.label: Dominant integral element

anchor.snippet: we say that an integral element

annotations.note: No notion of dominant integral element relative to a choice of positive roots is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Theorem of the highest weight

anchor.snippet: The irreducible finite-dimensional representations of K are then classified by a theorem of the highest weight

annotations.note: Loogle finds no `highest_weight` declaration; the highest weight classification theorem is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Character of a representation

anchor.snippet: we define the character of

annotations.note: `FDRep.character` defines the character g ↦ tr(ρ g) for finite-dimensional representations, but only the (discrete) group case — not the continuous compact-group setting.

annotations.label: Character is a class function

anchor.snippet: This function is easily seen to be a class function

annotations.note: `FDRep.char_conj` proves `V.character (h * g * h⁻¹) = V.character g`, capturing the class-function property in the discrete-group setting.

annotations.label: Characters form an orthonormal basis

anchor.snippet: the characters form an orthonormal basis for the set of square-integrable class functions in K

annotations.note: `FDRep.char_orthonormal` proves orthogonality of characters for irreducible representations of a finite group; the L²(K) statement for compact groups is not formalized.

annotations.label: Weyl character formula

anchor.snippet: the Weyl character formula , which gives an explicit formula for the character

annotations.note: Loogle finds no Weyl character formula declaration; Mathlib's `Weyl`-named decls are about Weyl groups of root systems, not characters.

annotations.label: Representations of SU(2)

anchor.snippet: We now consider the case of the compact group SU(2)

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Matrix.specialUnitaryGroup` but no developed representation theory of SU(2) (symmetric power representations, etc.).

annotations.label: Weyl character formula for SU(2)

anchor.snippet: the Weyl character formula says, in this case

annotations.note: The explicit SU(2) Weyl character formula is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weyl's proof of the character formula

anchor.snippet: The first major step in the argument is to prove the Weyl character formula

annotations.note: No formalization of Weyl's analytic proof of the character formula exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tannaka–Krein duality

anchor.snippet: The topic of recovering a compact group from its representation theory is the subject of the Tannaka–Krein duality

annotations.note: `TannakaDuality.FiniteGroup.equiv` proves Tannaka duality for finite groups (G ≃* Aut(forget k G)); the compact-group Tannaka–Krein theorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Weyl's unitarian trick

anchor.snippet: The influence of the compact group theory on non-compact groups was formulated by Weyl in his unitarian trick

annotations.note: Loogle finds no `unitarian` declaration; Weyl's unitarian trick is not formalized in Mathlib.
